A standard 2xBB raise to 200 (total) takes the initiative, preserves fold equity, builds a pot when I hit, and lets me play post‑flop in position. If he jams over the raise I’ll fold or reassess based on his shove frequency; if he calls or does a small 3‑bet I’ll proceed cautiously on the flop.

Heads-up against an aggressive Minni. Getting 3:1 pot odds with 97o. Minni's button raise has a wide range in this situation, so my hand has decent equity (~35-40%). The odds justify calling, and I can't afford to fold too tight with my shorter stack or I'll be blinded away. Calling allows me to see the flop and make a decision based on the action.

Weak 97o on 6-4-Q flop with minimal equity. Out of position against aggressive Minni in short stack situation. Check to control pot size and evaluate Minni's action without committing more chips to a marginal hand.

Heads‑up in position after I opened preflop. My 7♥8♦ on Q♥6♥4♦ has no pair but has a backdoor heart plus a gutshot to a 5 — some equity but not a made hand. Claude checked; he defended wide preflop but is often passive postflop. I want to use my position and initiative to take the pot down or get called by worse while preserving pot control. A half‑pot c‑bet (200 into 400) applies pressure to K/A/X hands that often fold, gives Claude about a 25% break‑even call threshold, and leaves room to continue barreling on favorable turns. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to a sizable check‑raise, continue cautiously if he just calls).

Weak 97o with no draws on 6-4-Q board versus aggressive short-stack opponent. While pot odds are 3:1 (needing 25% equity), my actual equity against Minni's range is likely 20-30% at best. Out of position with no draws and facing a marginal spot - must fold and preserve chips for better opportunities. Short stack should be selective, not desperate.

7-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. While heads-up play typically involves wider calling ranges, 7-2o remains a clear fold from the button even in this context. This hand has minimal equity and poor playability postflop. With 26 hands remaining in the tournament and a moderate stack (47 BB), I should fold weak hands and wait for premium opportunities. Minni has shown aggression throughout, making this hand particularly difficult to play. No value in committing chips to this spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 5s6s — a very playable, suited connector with great postflop potential and positional advantage. Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call; I want to take the initiative, build a pot with a hand that plays well multi-street, and apply pressure to Claude’s wide BB defending range. A minimum raise to 200 (total) is standard here: it’s small enough to keep worse hands and draws in, gives me fold equity, and keeps my range honest. Plan: c-bet many favorable flops, use position to realize equity on draws and straights/flushes, and fold to large, committed 3-bets (while calling smaller 3-bets to play postflop depending on sizing).

Folding 7-8 unsuited in the big blind. While pot odds of 3:1 appear attractive, this hand is too weak to defend. Playing out of position post-flop with marginal holdings against an aggressive opponent like Minni would put me in a difficult spot. With my short stack (4600), I need to preserve chips and wait for stronger hands where I have better equity and can make more profitable decisions. This is a disciplined fold.

I'm on the button with 8hJh against Minni in the big blind. This is a decent broadway hand with a flush draw possibility. In heads-up play from the button, I should be raising with this quality of hand to apply pressure and control the pot. I only need to put in 50 more to call, so raising to 200 (the minimum raise) is optimal here. This also helps disguise my play - I'll mix in raises with various hand strengths. Minni is a frequent aggressor, but having position and raising first gives me an initiative advantage. With stack consideration (4550 chips), playing aggressively with decent hands is necessary.

Claude opened to 200 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Q♦3♥ — a marginal hand but worth defending heads-up against a 2bb open. A 3-bet here (even a min-raise to 300) with Q3o is thin and can force me into difficult spots vs a shove or a strong comeback. Calling is cheap (100 more) and gives good pot odds while keeping the pot manageable out of position. Plan: see the flop, continue only with meaningful connection (Q, backdoor flush/straight possibilities, or good bluffing opportunities), otherwise fold to heavy action. Therefore I will call (total commitment 200).

I have Qd3h on Qs‑5d‑6d: top pair but a very weak kicker. Heads‑up this is still a strong holding and I want to both get value from worse Qx / small pairs and charge diamond/straight draws, while keeping the pot manageable against Claude’s short 4,400 stack. Betting also defines his range instead of giving him a free card OOP. A half‑pot lead (~200 into 400) accomplishes that — it extracts thin value, charges draws, and keeps my commitment reasonable if he shoves. If he jams I’ll re‑evaluate (his shove range at this stack size will be wide and include many draws/air), but for now I’ll put in a half‑pot bet to protect and get value.

I have J8 which is a reasonable hand in heads-up play against Minni's flop raise. With J-high and multiple drawing possibilities (gutshots, pair outs), I have approximately 35-45% equity. The pot odds are 3:1, and I only need 25% equity to call profitably. Calling allows me to see another card while preserving my stack. Folding is too tight with this hand, and raising overcommits chips when short-stacked against a much larger stack. I'll call 200 to continue.

Turn is Q♠‑5♦‑6♦‑9♦; I hold Q♦3♥ — top pair but a weak kicker, and I now have a one‑card diamond draw (4 diamonds between my hand and board). Claude called my flop raise, so his range contains pairs (9x/6x/5x), Qx, single diamond draws and the occasional two‑diamond combo. I want to charge draws and extract thin value from worse Qx / small pairs while keeping the pot manageable vs Claude’s 4,200 stack. A half‑pot bet (~400 into 800) accomplishes that: it denies a free river to many hands, gets called by draws and weaker pairs, and doesn’t commit me too heavily if he turns aggressive. If Claude jams big I’ll fold (my pair + weak kicker is vulnerable to made flushes/strong hands), but against a normal call or small raise I’m happy to continue. Bet 400.

On the turn with J8, I'm facing a 400 call into a 1500 pot. My equity with one card remaining is approximately 20-22% (primarily from straight outs with a 7, and pair outs). I need 25% equity to break even. The mathematics are marginal at best, and given my short stack (4200), folding here is correct. I preserve chips for a better spot with superior equity where my short stack can be used more effectively. Minni's turn action suggests a strong made hand rather than a draw.

Heads-up on the button (postflop position). K5s is a playable, suited King with good postflop playability and blocker value vs strong Kx/AK hands. With Claude relatively short-stacked (~41bb) and me deep, I want to open a wide, aggressive range to steal the blind and take advantage of position while keeping the pot controllable. A 3x open (300 total) is standard here — it preserves postflop maneuverability, gives fold equity versus marginal BB defenses, and isn’t so large that I’m committing unnecessarily or inviting frequent all-ins. Plan: open-raise to 300; if called, play aggressively with position and c-bet appropriate boards, fold to strong resistance when clearly beaten.

Q5o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant defending from the BB against Minni's raise, despite getting 2:1 pot odds. While Minni is aggressive and could have a wide range, Q5 is too marginal to play profitably post-flop out of position. With a shortened stack of 4100 chips and 23 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for premium hands where I have better equity. Folding here is the higher-EV play against an aggressive opponent with strong stack advantage.

Folding 6-10 offsuit from the button in a heads-up match with a short stack (4050). While I have 3:1 pot odds, my equity with this weak hand is marginal (approximately 25-30%) against Minni's wide opening range. With such a significant chip disadvantage, I need to be selective and wait for premium hands that give me genuine positive expectation. Preserving my stack for better spots is the correct long-term strategy.

Heads-up on the button with K9s — a strong, playable hand that benefits from playing aggressively in position. Limping/calling is passive and gives Claude cheap flops; raising applies pressure to his wide BB defending range and builds the pot when I have position. Standard sizing in this game is ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB). If Claude 3-bets or shoves I’ll re-evaluate based on his range and pot odds; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures and extract value from worse hands.

I'm in the big blind heads-up against Minni with Ac9d. Minni raised to 300 from the button. I need to call 200 more into a 400 chip pot, giving me 2:1 pot odds. 

Ac9d is a reasonable hand in heads-up play - it has decent equity against Minni's likely raising range. Given the pot odds and the fact that my stack is relatively short (3950), I should see a flop. Minni has been aggressive throughout this session and button raises in heads-up are typically quite wide, so my hand has decent relative value here.

I'll call to see the flop and evaluate further based on board texture and action.

Checking is the most appropriate play with Ac9d on QsJd8s. I have ace-high with some straight outs but a marginal hand overall. Being out of position and first to act with this holding, checking allows me to control the pot, see Minni's action, and make an informed decision. Betting puts me in a difficult spot out of position with a weak hand and short stack.

Heads-up on the button with K♠9♠ on Q♠ 8♦ J♠. I have the nut-spade draw (9 spade outs) plus three remaining kings = ~12 outs to at least pair or make the flush by river (~45% to improve by river, ~25.5% to hit on the turn). Against a wide BB checking range this is a textbook semi-bluff — I want fold equity against small pairs and missed overcards, to charge weaker spade draws and to build a pot when I have position and strong equity. Pot is 600, so I’ll size to ~2/3 pot (400). That size balances extracting value and getting callers I crush while not committing myself to a call of a huge shove. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet/raise when I hit, or barrel/controlsize on safe runouts); if he jams all-in (~3.75k) I’ll fold the flop bet because calling would demand ~70% equity, which my draw doesn’t provide.

Calling 400 into a 1000 pot with 3.5:1 odds. Ac9d on QsJd8s gives me 8 straight outs (K or T) plus ace high. Against Minni's likely wide continuation betting range from the button, this is positive equity. With a short stack, I need to play marginal spots with good pot odds. An open-ended straight draw combined with ace-high provides sufficient equity to justify the call.

Checking on the turn with Ac9d (ace-high + OESD) against Minni's aggression. The 2c was a blank for my hand. Being short-stacked and out of position, checking allows me to see Minni's action and evaluate whether I have the pot odds to call or fold. I have 8 straight outs plus ace-high which gives me reasonable equity if called, but need to preserve flexibility with my short stack.

Turn: I completed a very strong hand — K♠9♠ on Q♠ 8♦ J♠ 2♣ gives me a K‑high spade flush. Only A♠ currently beats me; full houses are possible only if Claude holds a pocket pair that pairs the river. Claude’s line (call flop, check turn) looks like a range of one‑pair hands (Qx/Jx), floaters, spade draws and some medium pairs — many of which will call a reasonable value bet. Pot is 1,400 and Claude has ~3,350 behind, so I should extract value now. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (900) to get called by worse made hands and weaker spade combos while not over‑betting and folding out marginal hands. If he check‑raises or shoves I’ll re‑evaluate (a shove often represents an A♠ or a very narrow value range), but this sizing maximizes value against his likely calling range.

Calling with Ac9d (OESD + ace-high) on QsJd8s2c against Minni's turn raise. I have 8 outs to the straight plus ace-high equity. Pot odds are 3.56:1 and I need approximately 22% equity to break even. With 8 outs (17% on river) plus ace-high (another 5-10%), I have roughly 25-28% equity, making this a marginal but positive EV call. In a short-stack scenario with 21 hands remaining, I need to accumulate chips and these odds justify the aggression.

Checking with queen-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q) on QsJd8s2cTs. I've made a very strong hand that beats most of Minni's likely holdings. Given her aggressive pattern throughout this hand and my short stack, checking induces a bet from her with weaker made hands. I can then call and win a larger pot. Betting all-in would only get called by full houses or fold, whereas checking allows value extraction from her entire betting range.

I have K♠9♠ — a king‑high spade flush on the river (only A♠ beats me). The T♠ on the river also completes several straight combinations (any 9 or A+K), but Claude’s line (call flop 400, call turn 900, then check river) is most consistent with one‑pair and medium‑pair hands (Qx/Jx/T/8x) or sticky pocket pairs — hands that will often call a small river value bet but are unlikely to have been calling big turn bets with only a flip/weak gutshot. A large river shove is unnecessary and invites being called by the few hands that beat me; checking back concedes value from many worse pairs. I’ll make a modest value bet (~1/3–2/5 pot) to target Q/J/T/8 pairs and similar holdings while keeping the price too small to overcommit to a raise. Bet 1,200 chips (~37.5% of the 3,200 pot). If Claude jams/raises big I’ll give him credit for the very small range that beats me (A♠/straight/full house) and fold; if he calls, I get value and go to showdown.

I have Ac9d on a board of Qs8dJs2cTs, which makes a queen-high straight (Q-J-T-9-8). This is an extremely strong hand that beats all non-full house holdings.

Minni has been aggressively betting throughout this hand and just raised to 1200 on the river after I checked. She's been driving the action with raises on every street (flop 400, turn 900, river 1200), suggesting she either has a very strong hand or is bluffing.

With a queen-high straight:
- I beat all single pairs and high card hands
- I beat weaker straights  
- I only lose to full houses (which would require her to hold QQ, JJ, or 88)

My stack is 2450, and I need to call 1200 to win a pot of 5600. This represents 4.67:1 pot odds.

Against her likely range given the aggressive betting pattern (which includes QQ, JJ, 88, AK, AQ, AJ, or bluffs), I have the best hand a significant portion of the time. While full houses are possible, they're unlikely given her preflop action - a huge overbet on river would suggest a very strong hand, but her bet sizing (1200 into 4400) is reasonable for value with most hands, including those weaker than mine.

Calling preserves my stack for future hands while winning the pot against most of her holdings. A queen-high straight is the strongest hand possible excluding full houses and royal flushes, making this a clear call.

I call 1200.

Calling with 8-7 suited on the button in heads-up. At 1200 chips with 20 hands left, I'm in critical survival mode and need to see flops with decent hands. 8-7 suited has good potential (flush/straight draws) and I'm getting 3:1 pot odds. Position advantage on button increases the value of this hand. Need to accumulate chips to stay viable for the final 20 hands.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a short-stacked SB (Claude ~1,150 ≈ 11.5bb). My hand 8♦2♥ is essentially junk — dominated and with almost no playability out of position. A min-raise to 200 will often induce a shove from a short stack, and calling that shove with 8-2 is a very poor spot despite the small absolute risk. I’ll preserve chips and control the pot size: check and see a flop. If Claude shoves I’ll fold (no reason to commit to a marginal call); if he checks or makes a small action I can proceed postflop with pot control.

I flopped a small pair (2♣? actually 2♦ on board and I hold 2♥ — bottom pair) but I’m out of position vs a very short-stacked SB (≈11.5bb). Pot is only 200. I don’t want to build the pot with a marginal hand that will be dominated a lot of the time or that I’ll have to call an all‑in with. Checking controls the pot size, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me fold comfortably to a shove. A shove by Claude (all‑in ~1150) would require ~46% equity to call (call 1150 to win ~1350) — his shove range is weighted toward hands that beat me (T/9/22/two pair) or have enough equity to make a call unprofitable. Plan: check and fold to a shove; if he bets a small amount I can call or re-evaluate, if he checks I’ll take a cheap turn card and re-assess.

Critical short stack (1150 chips) with 8c7c on 2d-9c-Tc. I have an open-ended straight draw plus flush draw (15 outs), which provides strong equity. Minni checked, showing weakness. I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips before the final hands. Betting 150 applies pressure while keeping 1000 chips for potential all-in on turn. This semi-bluff has both immediate fold equity and strong equity if called - exactly what a short-stack should want.

I have 8♦2♥ on 2♦ 9♣ T♣ — bottom pair. Pot is 350 and Claude (short ≈10bb) made a small flop stab of 150; calling 150 gives me ~30% pot odds. His range from a SB call preflop is wide (T/9, clubs draws, overcards, some bluffs), so my deuce has reasonable showdown value versus that range. I want to keep the pot controlled and realize equity rather than folding a made hand for cheap. If Claude jams later (commits his short stack) I’ll fold — but for this small bet I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have 8♦2♥ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) with an 8 kicker on 2♦ 9♣ T♣ K♣. The turn completed a three‑club board and brought a King, so many hands and draws beat or have outs against me (Kx, T/9, any two‑club combos that could already be a made flush, and single‑club hands with a river club out). Claude is very short (~1,000 ≈ 10bb) and is incentivized to shove light — his flop line (small stab) is consistent with a wide range including hands that already beat me or have good equity vs my pair. Calling a shove here would cost ~1,000 to win ~1,500 (~40% equity needed), which my bottom pair does not have versus his likely jam range. I want to preserve my deep stack and keep pots small with marginal hands. Best line is to check and let Claude show his hand: if he checks back I get a cheap river; if he fires a small bet I can call/re‑evaluate; if he shoves I fold. Pot control and folding to all‑in maximizes my long‑term EV here.

Betting 300 on the turn with 8c7c on 2d-9c-Tc-Kc board. I have 14 outs (6-7-8-9-T straight, 7-8-9-T-J straight, and flush outs) providing approximately 30% equity. With only 1000 chips remaining and 20 hands left, I'm in critical survival mode and must accumulate chips. Minni's back-to-back checks indicate a weak holding, making this an ideal spot to apply pressure. This bet:
- Wins the pot immediately against weak holdings or air
- Provides value if Minni calls with a marginal hand (I have genuine equity)
- Sets up a river shove with optimal chip commitment
- Avoids allowing Minni a free river where they could hit and beat me or win by check-back with a marginal hand
Leaving 700 chips allows flexibility on the river while maintaining pressure.

Board is 2♦ 9♣ T♣ K♣ and I hold 8♦2♥ — bottom pair on a three‑club board. Claude is very short (~700) and has shown a small flop stab then a modest turn bet (300). Calling 300 into the 800 pot gives me ~27% pot‑odds to see the river, and my deuce has reasonable showdown value vs the wide SB calling/shoving range (Kx/Tx/9x, some clubs and bluffs). Raising here would be unnecessary and often forces a shove I don’t want to call; folding now surrenders too much equity. Plan: call the 300 to control the pot and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to any shove that commits Claude’s remaining stack, call small river bets if appropriate, and only invest further with improved equity or a clear value spot.

I rivered trips (2♣? no — I hold 2♥ plus board 2♦/2♠ = trips). This is a very strong hand in heads‑up vs a very short SB (Claude ~700). The only realistic hands that beat me are club flushes, straights (QJ), or full houses/quads (pocket TT/99/KK etc.), but those are a minority of his call/raise line; his small stab bets on the flop/turn are consistent with a wide, often marginal range. With my huge effective stack advantage (38,200 vs 700) it is correct to extract value rather than protect – I want to price Claude all‑in and turn marginal callers into committed calls. Betting the size of his remaining stack forces a call/fold decision and maximizes EV; losing 700 occasionally to the rare flush/full house is acceptable given the frequency of worse hands that will call. Action: bet 700 (effectively putting him all‑in).

Critical river decision with 8c7c on 2d-9c-Tc-Kc-2s. I completely missed my draw - no straight, no flush, just 8-high. This is the worst possible outcome after my aggressive semi-bluff lines on the flop and turn. Minni's river raise after checking twice suggests either a made hand (likely a pair) or a bluff. However, my hand has essentially zero showdown value and cannot win against any legitimate holding. For this 700-chip all-in call, I need approximately 22% equity to break even (calling 700 to win ~2500). With 8-high, I likely have only 15-20% equity at best against Minni's range - a clear negative EV situation. Although I'm desperately short-stacked with ~20 hands remaining, spewing my last chips on a -EV call is not the answer. Better spots will come where I can commit my chips with actual hand strength or better equity. Folding preserves my 700 remaining chips to compete in upcoming hands.

Heads-up on the button with 9♦7♣ — a playable but marginal hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to complete to 100. Claude (BB) is very short (~600), so a min-raise to 200 will very likely induce an immediate shove; I don't want to commit a big portion of chips with a marginal offsuit hand and poor equity vs a shove range. By completing to 100 I keep the pot small, retain positional advantage postflop, and can fold or proceed based on the flop texture and his action. This is the higher EV, lower-variance play here.

AJ is a premium holding in heads-up play. With only 600 chips at 50/100 blinds, I'm in critical chip condition and being blinded away. I need to build my stack by winning pots with strong hands. A raise to 200 (min-raise) is aggressive enough to gain fold equity while preserving some flexibility, and it provides good value if Minni calls with a weaker hand.

Call to 150 (i.e. put 100 more). 9♦7♣ is marginal but playable heads-up in position; Claude is very short (~500) and his min-raise is likely trying to induce a shove. I don’t want to commit a large portion of my stack with a weak, offsuit hand — calling keeps the pot small, preserves positional advantage, and lets me continue only on favorable flops or fold to heavy action. If he jams after my call I’ll re-evaluate based on shove size and pot odds, but preflop 3-betting here is unnecessarily high-variance.

All-in shove with AJ on 4-8-4 flop. Severely short-stacked (500 chips) with pot at 400. AJ has decent equity with gutshot straight draw and backdoor flush draw. Check-calling would be exploitable; going all-in applies maximum pressure while leveraging fold equity and maintaining my equity in the hand. This is a critical survival spot requiring aggression.

Claude has shoved/risen all-in on the flop to 500 (his stack now 0). Pot is 900, so calling 500 wins a 1400 pot — break-even equity ≈ 500/1400 = 35.7%. With 9♦7♣ on 4♣ 8♠ 4♠ I’m currently behind most made hands (any 4, any 8, overpairs, big pairs) but I have ~6 clean outs to make a pair by the river and some small backdoor two‑pair/runner possibilities. Claude is very short and his shove will include a fair number of weaker hands and bluffs in addition to made hands, so my estimated equity vs his shoving range is around the break‑even point or slightly above. More importantly, the call is a tiny fraction of my 39,100 stack so the variance cost is low and this is a +EV, low-risk spot to call and realize equity. I’ll call and see the turn/river.
